insights.insight: Ad tech charges $5–$150 CPA on probabilistic attribution; this protocol settles verified purchase events at roughly $0.10 each — a 50–200x cost gap that isn't a pricing negotiation, it's a different category of signal.

With Chrome cookie deprecation now complete, the industry is patching with fingerprinting and first-party data — both partial and shrinking; a cryptographically verified receipt sidesteps the entire attribution stack because the purchase event is the proof, not an inference from it.

Amazon knows Amazon spend; Kroger knows Kroger spend; neither sees the other — this protocol captures both in the same dataset without attaching identity, which is the specific blind spot that no single first-party data program can fix.

The network's geographic footprint is building along a rural spine across 26 CBSAs — populations that are systematically underrepresented in cookie-based and first-party data programs, meaning the verified purchase signal is strongest exactly where ad tech coverage is weakest.

Top-brand receipt data already spans grocery (King Soopers, Kroger, H-E-B), mass retail (Walmart, Target), warehouse (Costco), and home improvement (Home Depot) — basket composition across categories is visible at the aggregate level without a loyalty card or pixel.

insights.topic: Velocity plateau as baseline integrity check
insights.insight: Token velocity has held flat at 0.58–0.63x for six consecutive measurement periods even as geographic reach expands — steady intake without artificial spikes is a data quality indicator brands should want before building targeting decisions on top of any new signal source.
